title: Seemingly shareable memory ports don't get merged
body:
I asked about this on the community slack and was directed to file this here. This may be WAI ,and just me failing to understand some verilog semantic that makes what I expect impossible/incorrect. Apologies if so.

The MCVE is a silly way to implement a memory. It's reduced out of a bigger design where I was hoping to get yosys to infer proper port widths for me, in a context where it's annoying to calculate and plumb them explicitly. If the resolution of this bug is "stop doing strange things", that's fair enough :)

### Version

Yosys 0.44 (git sha1 80ba43d26264738c93900129dc0aab7fab36c53f, g++ 13.3.0 -fPIC -O3)

### On which OS did this happen?

Linux

### Reproduction Steps

Implement a basic memory, but represented as an array of 1-bit regs instead of the word width:

```systemverilog
module bitram2x1(input CLK,
                 input [1:0] AD,
                 input [1:0] DI,
                 input WE,
                 output reg [1:0] DO);
  (* no_rw_check *)
  reg ram [7:0];

  integer word_addr = AD*2;

  always @(posedge CLK) begin
    if (WE) begin
      for (integer i = 0; i < 2; i=i+1) begin
         ram[word_addr+i] <= DI[i];
      end
    end

    for (integer i = 0; i < 2; i=i+1) begin
      DO[i] <= ram[word_addr+i];
    end
  end
endmodule
```

Load into yosys and do some basic synth passes. Both these scripts produce the issue. First attempt with just basic generic synthesis:

```
read_verilog -sv weirdram.v; prep; dump -o weirdram.rtlil
```

Then with explicitly trying all the resource sharing passes:

```
read_verilog -sv weirdram.v; proc; flatten; wreduce;;; share; memory_share; opt_merge; memory_collect; dump -o weirdram.rtlil
```

I also tried removing `no_rw_check` and varying the module's port widths. This behavior seems consistent in all the variations I tried, up to 18b wide DI/DO.

### Expected Behavior

I expected both the 2 one-bit write ports and the 2 one-bit read ports to get merged, resulting in a `$mem_v2` with a single read port and a single write port, both 2 bits wide.

### Actual Behavior

The ports remain un-merged, the `$mem_v2` shows 2 one-bit read ports and two one-bit write ports. `show` confirms that the pre- and post-logic to generate multiple copies of addresses and mux single bits back together remains in the design.

Notable in the log is that `memory_collect` reports that the ports cannot be shared by address, despite always accessing an adjacent pair of bits. SAT sharing also says that sharing is impossible, but I failed to find a way to make it describe the failure further, so that may be expected.

```
7. Executing MEMORY_SHARE pass (consolidating $memrd/$memwr cells).
Consolidating read ports of memory bitram2x1.ram by address:
Consolidating write ports of memory bitram2x1.ram by address:
Consolidating write ports of memory bitram2x1.ram using sat-based resource sharing:
  Checking group clocked with posedge \CLK, width 1: ports 0, 1.
  Common input cone for all EN signals: 1 cells.
  Size of unconstrained SAT problem: 9 variables, 18 clauses
  According to SAT solver sharing of port 0 with port 1 is not possible.
```
code_blocks:
module bitram2x1(input CLK,
                 input [1:0] AD,
                 input [1:0] DI,
                 input WE,
                 output reg [1:0] DO);
  (* no_rw_check *)
  reg ram [7:0];

  integer word_addr = AD*2;

  always @(posedge CLK) begin
    if (WE) begin
      for (integer i = 0; i < 2; i=i+1) begin
         ram[word_addr+i] <= DI[i];
      end
    end

    for (integer i = 0; i < 2; i=i+1) begin
      DO[i] <= ram[word_addr+i];
    end
  end
endmodule
read_verilog -sv weirdram.v; proc; flatten; wreduce;;; share; memory_share; opt_merge; memory_collect; dump -o weirdram.rtlil

7. Executing MEMORY_SHARE pass (consolidating $memrd/$memwr cells).
Consolidating read ports of memory bitram2x1.ram by address:
Consolidating write ports of memory bitram2x1.ram by address:
Consolidating write ports of memory bitram2x1.ram using sat-based resource sharing:
  Checking group clocked with posedge \CLK, width 1: ports 0, 1.
  Common input cone for all EN signals: 1 cells.
  Size of unconstrained SAT problem: 9 variables, 18 clauses
  According to SAT solver sharing of port 0 with port 1 is not possible.
